title: How to Coach — Not Just What to Coach
intro:
APPENDIX C · THE COACH’S TOOLKIT

How to Coach — Not Just What to Coach

Mission: Make the coach as systematic as the system. A great curriculum coached badly produces nothing. A modest curriculum coached well produces champions.

AUDIENCE
COACH / SM
PRIMARY SKILL
SBI FEEDBACK

ADVISOR Δ / QTR

Five Principles The SBI Framework The Coaching Conversation Calibration Psychological Safety Difficult Conversations Train-the-Trainer The Coach’s Own KPIs

sections.title: The Five Principles of Coaching at HAB
sections.html:
The Five Principles of Coaching at HAB

If the curriculum is the “what” and the 90-Day Plan is the “when,” the Coach’s Toolkit is the “how.” These five principles govern every coaching interaction — ride-along, 1:1, debrief, and hot in-the-moment coaching on the floor.

PRINCIPLE 1

Specific Beats Sincere

“Great call” rewires nothing. “You held the pause for a full two seconds — that’s Level-2 mastery” rewires fast. Always name the observable behavior.

PRINCIPLE 2

One Thing At a Time

An advisor can only consciously work on one new behavior per week. Pick the highest-leverage one. Park the rest. Trying to fix three things at once fixes none of them.

PRINCIPLE 3

Behavior, Not Identity

“You skipped Risk on that PRS” is coachable. “You’re bad at PRS” is an identity attack. The first changes behavior. The second changes employment.

PRINCIPLE 4

Praise In Public, Correct In Private

Public praise multiplies. Public correction shuts the team down for a week. This is not a soft rule — it’s a math rule about how feedback compounds.

PRINCIPLE 5

Coach the Process, Score the Outcome

The advisor controls behavior. The advisor doesn’t control whether a specific customer says yes. Coach the inputs (PRS used, micro-questions asked, base order). Score the outputs (authorization rate). Never reverse this.

PRINCIPLE 6

Coach the Rare Skill, Not the Easy One

Most coaches default to praising the things that come easy to the advisor. Praise the hard ones — the listening, the silence, the discipline of no kitchen-sink. The rare skill is where the next level lives.

sections.title: The SBI Framework — The Coaching Sentence That Always Works
sections.html:
The SBI Framework — The Coaching Sentence That Always Works

Every coaching note, every 1:1, every ride-along debrief uses the same three-part sentence: Situation, Behavior, Impact. Use it for praise. Use it for correction. The structure removes ambiguity and protects the relationship.

Figure C.1 · SBI — the three-part sentence that makes every coaching note useful
TypeVague (don’t do this)SBI (do this)
Praise “Great call!” “On the 11am call (S), you said the investment number and went immediately into the warranty without pausing (B). The customer didn’t flinch and authorized in the next breath (I). That’s the Level-3 close I’ve been looking for.”
Correction “You need to do better on objections.” “On the 2pm call with the spouse objection (S), you went straight to discounting instead of offering the three-way call (B). That sets a precedent that complaining gets discounts, and it killed our chance to talk to the actual decision-maker (I). Let’s drill Boss 4 together this afternoon.”

sections.title: The Coaching Conversation — Five Beats, Twelve Minutes
sections.html:
The Coaching Conversation — Five Beats, Twelve Minutes

Every 1:1 and every post-ride-along debrief runs on the same five-beat structure. It scales from a 5-minute hot debrief to a full 30-minute quarterly check-in. The order matters — especially Beats 1 and 5.

BeatWhatSample WordsTime
1Open with Trust. Two minutes of human. No agenda.“How’s your week going?”2 min
2Praise (SBI). One specific behavior from the past week. Always.“On Tuesday’s call you…”2 min
3One Correction (SBI). One. Highest-leverage. Get advisor’s read first.“Walk me through how that call landed for you…”4 min
4Commit to One Behavior. Specific. Observable. By next 1:1.“Next week, on every PRS, you’ll…”2 min
5Close with Belief. Tell them you see their next level.“You’re one cycle away from Pro.”2 min
PRO TIPGet the advisor’s read before you give yours.

In Beat 3, ALWAYS ask the advisor what they thought of the call/behavior before you offer your view. Two reasons: they often already know what went wrong (so you’re reinforcing self-coaching, not lecturing), and they hear correction from themselves far better than from you. Coaching is mostly Socratic.

sections.title: Calibration — Making Sure “Mastery” Means the Same Thing Across Coaches
sections.html:
Calibration — Making Sure “Mastery” Means the Same Thing Across Coaches

If you have multiple coaches (or an owner + a manager both coaching), calibration becomes the silent killer. Two coaches grading the same call as “4/5” vs. “2/5” means there is no shared standard — which means promotions feel arbitrary, which means trust in the system erodes. Run calibration sessions monthly.

COACH’S CORNERThe Monthly Calibration Drill

Setup: Pick three recorded calls representing a range (one great, one middle, one weak). All coaches score independently using the Ride-Along Scorecard.

Reveal: Coaches read their scores aloud. Where the spread is > 1 point, discuss the specific moment that drove the variance. Agree on a shared rubric.

Outcome: Within three months of monthly calibration, inter-coach variance should drop below 0.5 points. That’s when promotions become trustworthy.

sections.title: Psychological Safety — The Coaching Substrate
sections.html:
Psychological Safety — The Coaching Substrate

Advisors will not surface mistakes, ask for help, or try new behaviors if they fear being shamed for them. The coach’s most invisible job is to maintain the conditions under which advisors are willing to be imperfect. Lose this and the coaching system collapses no matter how good the curriculum is.

Behavior That Builds SafetyBehavior That Destroys Safety
Coach admits their own past mistakes openly.Coach talks about other advisors’ mistakes by name.
Correction always in private, paired with belief.Correction in the bullpen for the team to overhear.
“What were you trying to do?” before “here’s what I saw.”“Why did you…” (puts advisor on defense).
Praise the experiment, even when it failed.Punish every miss equally regardless of intent.
One coachable thing per 1:1. Compounding.Dump every backlog item into one conversation.

sections.title: Difficult Conversations — The Advisor Who Won’t Improve
sections.html:
Difficult Conversations — The Advisor Who Won’t Improve

Every coach will face the advisor who attends drills, nods along, and changes nothing. This is not a coaching problem — it’s a clarity problem. Run this conversation, in this order, exactly once:

  1. Name the pattern, not the person. “Over the last three weeks, I’ve scored PRS Completeness at 60%, 55%, 50%. That’s a trend, and it’s gone the wrong direction.”
  2. Ask what’s in the way. “What’s making this hard?” Then actually listen for 30 seconds without interrupting. Sometimes it’s a personal issue, a tool problem, or a misunderstanding of the standard.
  3. Make the standard explicit and observable. “The standard for this role is 100% PRS on every finding. Here’s what that looks like in practice. Show me what gets in the way of that on the next three calls.”
  4. Set a clear 30-day improvement gate. “In 30 days, the metric needs to be Green for two consecutive weeks. Here’s the support I’ll provide: [specific drills, ride-alongs, 1:1 cadence]. Here’s what happens if it doesn’t: [next step in your process].”
  5. Document, then follow through. Either the advisor improves — in which case you praise the climb out — or they don’t, in which case the conversation you’ve already had becomes the foundation for the next step. Coaching that has no consequence isn’t coaching — it’s wishing.
STOPNever have this conversation in the bullpen.

Office with the door closed, or off-site if necessary. Public versions of this conversation are humiliating for the advisor, terrifying for the team, and destroy psychological safety across the whole shop.

sections.title: Train-the-Trainer — Making the System Scale Past One Coach
sections.html:
Train-the-Trainer — Making the System Scale Past One Coach

The single biggest risk to the HAB Sales OS at any individual shop is “the coach got hit by a bus” — or, more commonly, got promoted, got pulled into operations, or left for another shop. Inside Phase 3 of the rollout, begin developing a second coach. Inside the first year, every shop should have a backup coach who can run the full cadence solo for two weeks.

StageBackup Coach DoesLead Coach Does
Month 1Shadows every drill, ride-along, and 1:1.Runs everything. Explains the “why” after each session.
Month 2Co-runs the daily drill. Co-scores ride-alongs. Calibration session weekly.Hands off the drill leadership. Audits the scoring.
Month 3Owns 50% of 1:1s. Owns the daily drill solo.Owns the other 50%. Reviews backup’s coaching notes.
Month 4+Runs solo for one full week (lead is “away”).Reviews KPIs at end of the week. Course-corrects if needed.

sections.title: The Coach’s Own KPIs
sections.html:
The Coach’s Own KPIs

The coach is measured on advisor improvement — not on personal sales, not on operational firefighting. These three KPIs go on the same wall as the shop dashboard. The coach is accountable to them quarterly.

Avg Advisor Level Change / Quarter
≥ 0.5 levels
Across the whole advisor team, the average mastery level rises at least half a level per quarter.
Coaching Notes Delivered / Week
5 × advisor count
One specific (SBI-format) note per advisor per workday. If this drops, everything downstream drops.
Calibration Variance
< 0.5 pts
If multi-coach: agreement on scored calls within half a point on the 1–5 scale.
THE COACH’S OATHA second oath, paired with the HAB Oath.

“I will praise the specific behavior, not the vague feeling. I will correct in private, never in the bullpen. I will coach the process and score the outcome. I will protect my team’s safety to try, fail, and try again. I will hold the cadence even when the shop is on fire. And I will measure my success not by what I do — but by what they become.”
